blocks.text: If $z$ is a function of $x$ and $y$, i.e., $z = f(x, y)$, the total differential $dz$ is obtained from the partial differentials $dx$ and $dy$ by the use of the following relation:

blocks.description: This equation describes how the total differential of a function can be expressed in terms of its partial derivatives with respect to its variables.

blocks.text: The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of location in space, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like $x$ and $y$.

blocks.caption: Figure 21: Change in position on a surface.
blocks.description: This figure illustrates how a point moves from position $P$ to position $Q$ on the surface, demonstrating the relationship between the rates of change of $x$ and $y$ as the point moves along the surface.

blocks.latex: \text{change in } z = \text{change in } x \cdot \text{change in } y
blocks.description: This expression emphasizes the relationship between changes in variables and the resulting change in the function value.

blocks.text: Let P be a point $(x, y)$ on a surface, and let P move to a new position Q near to P on the surface. The change of position is most easily represented by a diagram (Fig. 21).
